title: North American Wild Mushrooms Pack
source: North American Mycological Association & SOS Field Library

content.summary: Distinctive white cascading toothy mushroom growing on hardwood trees.
content.description: Produces a compact, unbranched fruiting body with long, soft, icicle-like spines (teeth) hanging downwards. Pure white when fresh, aging to pale cream or yellow.
content.identificationGuide: Look for dense white icicle-like teeth (1-5 cm long) hanging from a single unbranched bulbous base attached directly to hardwood trees (oak, beech, maple).
content.habitatDesc: Grows singly on decaying or living hardwood trees, particularly oak, beech, and maple.
content.toxicityDesc: Non-toxic. High culinary and medicinal value.
content.medicinalDesc: Contains erinacines and hericenones known to stimulate nerve growth factor (NGF) synthesis and support cognitive health.

sourcesAttribution.notes: Used historically as a tonic for digestion and vitality.

media.caption: Cascading white teeth on hardwood trunk

relationships.notes: Coral Tooth Fungus (Hericium coralloides) is also edible but has branched branches.

content.summary: Prized funnel-shaped yellow mushroom with false gill folds and apricot aroma.
content.description: Bright golden-yellow to orange cap shaped like a vase or trumpet. Underneath cap features blunt, cross-veined false gills running down the stem (decurrent). Fruit-like apricot smell.
content.identificationGuide: Differentiate from toxic Jack-o'-Lantern (Omphalotus olearius) by checking for FALSE GILLS (blunt ridges that cross-branch and do not peel cleanly) and solid white interior flesh.
content.habitatDesc: Forms mycorrhizal relationships with mossy oak, beech, and coniferous forests.
content.toxicityDesc: Edible and delicious when thoroughly cooked.
content.medicinalDesc: Rich in Vitamin D2, essential amino acids, and beta-glucans.

media.caption: Golden Chanterelle showing blunt false gills

references.title: Audubon Society Field Guide to North American Mushrooms

relationships.notes: Jack-o'-Lantern mushroom has true blade-like gills, grows in dense clusters on wood, and has orange interior flesh. POISONOUS.

content.summary: Highly prized spring mushroom with honeycomb pitted cap and completely hollow interior.
content.description: Distinctive elongated conical cap covered in dark brown to black pitted ridges resembling a honeycomb. The cap is fully attached to the stem at the base, and the entire mushroom is completely hollow inside.
content.identificationGuide: ALWAYS slice vertically in half from top to bottom. True morels are COMPLETELY HOLLOW from cap apex down through the stem base.
content.habitatDesc: Appears in spring around ash, tulip poplar, apple orchards, and recently burned forest areas.
content.toxicityDesc: MUST BE COOKED thoroughly. Contains toxic hydrazine-like compound when raw.
content.medicinalDesc: Rich in antioxidants and trace minerals.

sourcesAttribution.notes: Must verify hollow stem vs Gyromitra false morels.

media.caption: Honeycomb pitted cap of Black Morel

relationships.notes: False Morel (Gyromitra esculenta) has a brain-like wrinkled cap, is NOT hollow inside (filled with cottony tissue), and contains DEADLY gyromitrin toxin.

content.summary: Vibrant orange and yellow bracket fungus with pore underside and chicken-like texture.
content.description: Large overlapping shelf-like brackets ranging from vibrant yellow to bright orange. Underneath surface consists of tiny sulfur-yellow pores (no gills). Succulent fleshy texture reminiscent of cooked chicken breast.
content.identificationGuide: Look for bright orange/yellow shelf brackets on hardwood trees with yellow porous undersides. Avoid specimens growing on conifers or eucalyptus which can cause gastrointestinal distress.
content.habitatDesc: Grows on living or dead hardwood trees, especially oak.
content.toxicityDesc: Edible and delicious. Cook thoroughly. Test small portion first as a minority of individuals report mild digestive sensitivity.

media.caption: Vibrant orange shelves on oak trunk

references.title: Field Guide to Mushrooms of North America

content.summary: DEADLY POISONOUS mushroom responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide.
content.description: Smooth olive-green to yellowish cap, white free gills, skirt-like ring on upper stem, and a prominent cup-like volva sac enclosing the base of the stem (often buried under soil/duff).
content.identificationGuide: ALWAYS dig out the entire stem base when identifying white-gilled mushrooms to inspect for a buried cup-like volva sac and skirt ring.
content.habitatDesc: Mycorrhizal under hardwood trees, especially oak, beech, and chestnut.
content.toxicityDesc: DEADLY. Contains potent amatoxins that destroy liver and kidney tissue. Thermal cooking does NOT neutralize amatoxins.

media.caption: Death Cap showing volva cup at stem base

relationships.notes: Paddy Straw Mushroom (Volvariella volvacea) is confused by novice foragers, causing fatal intoxications.

stem.name: volva
stem.label: Skirt Ring & Sac Volva at Base

content.summary: Traditional medicinal polypore growing exclusively on birch trees, famous for historical usage.
content.description: Smooth, hoof-shaped or rubbery rounded bracket fungus with greyish-brown upper surface and white pore underside. Grows exclusively on birch trunks.
content.identificationGuide: Strict host association with Birch trees (Betula spp.). Smooth tan cap with white pore underside and tough corky interior.
content.habitatDesc: Exclusively on dead or living Birch trees.
content.toxicityDesc: Non-toxic, but too tough/corky for culinary ingestion.
content.medicinalDesc: Historically used as an antiparasitic, wound plaster, tinder lighter, and immune stimulant. Found in the pouch of Ötzi the Iceman (5,300 BP).

sourcesAttribution.notes: Used as natural tinder, band-aid strip, and parasite cleanser.

media.caption: Birch Polypore bracket on birch trunk

cap_color_gilled.question: What color is the cap surface?

host_tree_poroid.question: Where is the porous fungus growing?

options.label: Oak or Hardwood stumps as bright orange shelves
